regime_classification.2026-04-09:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: manufacturing fell to 53.9 from 56.9 in a single month, and core sector output decelerated to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% — a 240bps miss that cement and steel dispatches confirm. GDP at 7.8% is real but decelerating from 8.4%, and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% implies the statistical agency and the central bank are looking at different economies. Invert the bull case: if IIP capital goods at 12.5% is genuine investment acceleration, capacity utilisation should be rising — but the data is unavailable, which is itself a signal that the number may not flatter.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% masks a dangerous internal rotation: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month while fuel deflation of -3.78% is doing the statistical heavy lifting. Core CPI at 3.4% is flat — which means the moment fuel stops deflating, headline CPI has a straight path to 4.5%+ with Brent at $98. RBI's own inflation forecast has already revised up to 4.6% from 4.2% — if they cut 50bps into this trajectory, the second-order effect is INR depreciation feeding imported food inflation back into CPI with a two-quarter lag.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8%, a 300bps divergence that has widened for consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the first visible casualty of wholesale funding strain; corporate bond issuance at ₹1,12,000 crore confirms banks are rationing credit and borrowers are seeking alternatives. Last time India sustained a CD ratio above 80% during a rate-cut cycle — Q2 FY14 — the taper tantrum forced RBI to reverse with 75bps of emergency hikes within 12 months; today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but the structural tension is identical.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive enough to justify a cut, but Brent at $98 and food inflation accelerating to 3.47% give the MPC legitimate cover to wait. Markets are pricing 50bps of cuts within two quarters, which would compress the India-US rate differential from 190bps to 140bps — at a moment when FII equity flows have swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month. Cutting into a widening CAD, a depreciating INR at the 84th percentile, and a CD ratio at its highest stress level since 2022 is a policy combination that requires a very high conviction on disinflation holding.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY — up sharply from 4.3% — is the most encouraging number in today's dataset, but core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal) directly contradicts it: you cannot have genuine capex acceleration without core sector throughput. Real estate launches at 126,265 units — 0th percentile over a decade, an all-time low — despite an affordability index improving to 0.61 and home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1%, which suggests developers are not convinced demand is durable enough to commit capital. Munger's inversion: if private capex were genuinely accelerating, GST collections on capex-intensive sectors would be outpacing the headline ₹2,00,640 crore — they are not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore crossing the ₹2 lakh crore threshold is structurally significant, but the Mishra question is whether this reflects genuine demand expansion or formalization reclassifying existing informal spend — two-wheeler sales at 28.68% YoY and CV sales at 28.89% suggest rural and freight demand is genuinely strong, not just a base effect. Passenger vehicle sales at 26.1% YoY confirm urban discretionary is holding, but airline passenger traffic at the 7th percentile and e-commerce GMV growth decelerating from 25% to 12.4% suggest the upper-middle consumer is pulling back. Retail mall vacancy rising to 5.7% from 5.1% while e-commerce decelerates simultaneously is the most underappreciated signal: neither physical nor digital retail is accelerating — consumption breadth is narrower than the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI Composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: Manufacturing PMI fell to 53.9 from 56.9, core sector decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%, and airline passenger traffic dropped 9% to a 18-month low — none of which is consistent with a 7.8% economy firing on all cylinders. Invert the GDP print: the 170bps gap between the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% and the reported 7.8% demands a deflator explanation, not a celebration. What breaks this narrative is simple — if IIP capital goods momentum (up 12.5% this month but from a 4.3% prior base) doesn't sustain, the growth story reverts to its GVA skeleton, which is structurally closer to 6.5%.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% is benign, but the composition is a slow-motion trap: food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single print, FAO Food Index is at 128.5 (a 2.5% MoM rise), and Brent at $96.44 means the fuel deflation buffer of -3.78% is arithmetically finite — one administered price revision ends it. Core CPI is sticky at 3.4%, unchanged for two consecutive months, which tells you demand-side pricing power hasn't broken despite the rate cycle. Munger's inversion: if RBI cuts into a food-inflation re-acceleration while Brent adds $15bn to the import bill for every $10 move, the second-order effect is a CPI overshoot toward 5% by Q3 FY27 — precisely when the rate cut transmission would be peaking.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its 10-year range — is the single most important number in today's dataset, and it is moving in the wrong direction from 82.5%. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the classic early signal of wholesale funding stress; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore sits at the 7th percentile — a decade low — meaning corporates are not accessing bond markets as a relief valve, they are rationing debt entirely. Historically, the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14; within four quarters, the taper tantrum forced 75bps of emergency RBI hikes — the setup today rhymes, except the external trigger this time is Brent, not Fed tapering.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real policy rate of approximately +200bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates have averaged 100-150bps. RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the cutting room: a 25bps cut would still leave real rates above neutral, but the CD ratio at 83% means any rate cut that stimulates credit demand without a corresponding deposit response risks a macro-prudential response — higher risk weights on unsecured lending, as seen in November 2023. Home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1% have already improved the affordability index to 0.61 from 0.46 — the transmission is working in housing; the question is whether the RBI can hold this line if food inflation prints above 4% in May.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at +12.5% YoY looks strong until you note it follows a +4.3% prior print — the base effect is doing most of the work, not a genuine acceleration in investment orders. Core sector at 2.3% — covering steel, cement, electricity, and coal — is the most reliable capex proxy India has, and at an 18-month low, it contradicts the capital goods IIP entirely; cement dispatch and steel production don't lie about construction activity the way a single-month IIP print can. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable today, but auto and cement order book commentary points to plateauing utilisation — and Munger's question is the right one: if utilisation isn't above 75-80%, the incentive for private greenfield capex simply doesn't exist, regardless of what the budget allocates.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.006 lakh crore are a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: at this level, a meaningful share of the YoY gain reflects formalization reclassification — informal-to-formal migration — rather than net new demand creation, particularly as e-commerce GMV growth has halved from 25% to 12.4% YoY, the 15th percentile of its range. Vehicle sales tell a bifurcated story: PV at +26.1%, two-wheelers at +28.68%, and CVs at +28.89% all signal broad-based demand — but airline passenger traffic at 35.08 lakh, down 9% and at a 7th-percentile low, suggests urban discretionary spending is softening at the margin. Invert the GST record: if consumption were genuinely broad-based, e-commerce GMV and airline traffic — the two most real-time consumption proxies — would not both be printing multi-year lows simultaneously.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% looks unimpeachable until you decompose it: PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, PMI Manufacturing dropped to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% — the sharpest single-month deceleration in 18 months. IIP capital goods at 12.5% is a base-effect mirage, not organic acceleration; the prior month was 4.3%, and without capacity utilisation data to anchor it, the number is a headline without a foundation. Invert the bull case: if PMI sub-indices are rolling over while GDP is still printing 7.8%, the statistical machinery is running ahead of the real economy — and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% is a quiet admission that the gap closes downward, not upward.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is benign in aggregate, but the composition is shifting uncomfortably: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, while core CPI holds at 3.4% — meaning the headline is being held down entirely by fuel deflation at -3.78%, a subsidy-and-crude-price gift that reverses the moment Brent stabilises above $95. WPI at 2.13% and rising, FAO food index at 128.5 (up from 125.3), and copper at a 10-year high of $5.868/lb are the upstream signals that CPI is borrowing tranquility from a fuel line that won't stay negative. If RBI cuts into a food-inflation uptick, the second-order effect is INR carry unwind → imported inflation feedback → CPI back above 4.5% within two quarters.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of the past decade — with credit growing at 13.8% YoY against deposit growth of only 10.8%, a 300bps divergence widening for three consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the canary in the wholesale funding mine; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore confirms that large borrowers are bypassing banks entirely — which means the residual bank credit is increasingly concentrated in retail and SME, precisely the segments with the least collateral buffer. Last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% while the system was simultaneously absorbing NBFC stress was Q3 FY19 — within six months, IL&FS triggered a liquidity seizure that took 200bps of NBFC spreads wider and forced RBI into emergency OMOs; the setup today has better FX reserves but the same structural imbalance.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of 204bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates sit around 100-150bps. RBI's inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which arithmetically compresses the case for cuts even as the growth narrative invites them — the committee is caught between a headline that permits easing and an upstream commodity complex that punishes it. For housing: the average home loan rate has already fallen to 8.0% from 9.1%, affordability index improved to 0.61 from 0.46 — yet real estate sales are at an all-time low of 101,675 units against all-time high launches of 126,265 units, suggesting rate transmission to demand is broken, not lagged.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% is the number that will be cited in every bull deck today — and it is the number most likely to mislead. Core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal combined) is the physical proxy for actual investment activity, and it is decelerating sharply from 4.7%; you cannot have genuine capex acceleration when the materials that build factories are growing at a 10-quarter low. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable — and RBI's deliberate silence on this metric, given that sub-70% utilisation historically precedes private capex stagnation, is itself a signal. Munger's inversion: if private capex were genuinely accelerating, cement dispatches and steel consumption would confirm it — they do not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.01 lakh crore in the month — the 84th percentile — are the single most credible consumption signal in India's data architecture, because they cannot be revised away or deflated by a convenient price index. Vehicle sales corroborate: PV at +26.1% YoY, two-wheelers at +28.68% YoY, commercial vehicles at +28.89% YoY — a broad-based surge that, unlike PMI, is a hard transaction count. But invert: airline passenger volumes fell to 35.08 mn from 38.59 mn, e-commerce GMV growth collapsed to 12.4% from 25%, and retail mall vacancy is rising to 5.7% — the urban discretionary consumer who shops online and flies is softening, while the semi-urban buyer of a two-wheeler is surging; this is a consumption rotation, not a consumption broadening, and it has different implications for margin profiles across the consumption universe.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the quality test is in the decomposition. PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, Manufacturing slid to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — a 240bps miss that no press release will mention. IIP capital goods at 12.5% looks like acceleration until you note it is bouncing off a 4.3% base and capacity utilisation data is unavailable — the number flatters to deceive. Invert the bull case: if this growth is consumption-led (GST strong, PV sales up 26%) but investment-led indicators are softening, the next quarter's GDP will depend on whether the consumer keeps spending — and affordability data suggests she is being stretched.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is within the RBI's band, but the direction matters more than the level — food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, and the FAO food index is at 128.5, up from 125.3. Core CPI is flat at 3.4%, which is the number the RBI actually watches — and at 3.4% with a repo rate of 5.25%, the real policy rate is a restrictive 185bps. The Munger inversion: if the RBI cuts while food inflation is re-accelerating, the second-order effect is a weaker INR feeding imported inflation back into CPI with a two-quarter lag — the very outcome the cut was meant to avoid.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — 81st percentile, highest in this cycle — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposits at 10.8%, a 300bps divergence widening for three consecutive months. NBFC credit growth has decelerated sharply to 13% from 17%, the tell that wholesale funding stress is already transmitting. Corporate bond issuance at ₹112,000 cr sits at the 7th percentile — the lowest in a decade — meaning banks are rationing credit and forcing corporates to capital markets at precisely the moment those markets are pricing in rate cuts that may not arrive. The last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% while the repo rate was being cut was Q2 FY14; within 12 months, the taper tantrum forced RBI to reverse with 75bps of emergency hikes — today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but the structural tension rhymes.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of 204bps — restrictive by any measure, yet the RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the forward real rate and narrows the cutting window. At 8% average home loan rates (down from 9.1%), housing affordability is at its worst-ever reading of 0.61 — rate cuts have not cleared the affordability problem because prices have risen faster than the rate relief. For corporate capex, a 5.25% repo with a CD ratio at 83% means the marginal cost of bank funding is rising even as the policy rate holds — the transmission is broken in the direction that matters.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY is the number the bulls will cite; core sector at 2.3% — down from 4.7% — is the number that matters. Core sector covers eight infrastructure industries (steel, cement, electricity, coal, crude, refinery, fertiliser, natural gas) that account for 40% of IIP weight; when core sector and capital goods diverge by this magnitude, the capital goods print is likely driven by defence or electronics imports, not domestic industrial investment. PMI Manufacturing new orders are weakening (53.9 from 56.9), and without capacity utilisation data, the honest answer is: we cannot confirm whether this capex is expansion or replacement. Munger's test — if private capex were genuinely accelerating, cement dispatches and steel consumption would confirm it; they are not.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-12:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-12","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 cr is a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: April advance filings and the base effect of formalisation reclassifying informal spend can explain a significant portion of any GST beat — this is not purely demand. Vehicle sales are unambiguously strong — PV up 26.1%, two-wheelers up 28.68%, commercial vehicles up 28.89% — but airline passenger traffic fell to the 7th percentile at 35.08 mn, down from 38.59, and e-commerce GMV growth collapsed to 12.4% from 25%. The divergence between physical goods (strong) and discretionary services (weakening) suggests the consumption surge is concentrated in the mass-market durable segment — not the broad-based demand the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-13:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-13","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"India's headline GDP at 7.8% flatters on first read — but PMI Composite has slipped from 58.9 to 56.5 in a single month, manufacturing PMI dropped 300bps to 53.9, and core sector growth halved to 2.3% from 4.7%. regime_classification.2026-04-19:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-19","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 cr is a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: how much is genuine demand versus formalisation reclassifying existing spend, and how much is April advance filings inflating the month? regime_classification.2026-04-20:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-20","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the inversion is that PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, manufacturing slid to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — the slowest in 18 months. regime_classification.2026-04-20:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-20","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25%, unchanged, with RBI's own inflation forecast at 4.6% — the real rate is approximately 105bps positive, which is neither accommodative nor restrictive enough to resolve the CD ratio stress. Iron ore surging 54% in a month to $161.91 is a global commodity move, not an India capex signal — and CV sales at +28.89% reflect replacement demand and logistics recovery, not new plant and machinery investment. Private capex acceleration requires sustained capacity utilisation above 75% and visible order book depth; neither condition is currently evidenced.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-06-04:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-06-04","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore is a record, but the composition demands scrutiny: April advance filings and the formalization of previously informal transactions routinely inflate May GST prints, and the 71st-percentile reading is less exceptional once seasonality is stripped. Vehicle sales are genuinely strong — PV at 26.1%, 2W at 28.68%, CV at 28.89% — but airline passenger traffic at 35.08 million is a 7th-percentile decade-low, down 7.5% from the prior month, and e-commerce GMV growth has halved from 25% to 12.4% YoY. The divergence is a segmentation story: durable goods demand (financed, aspirational) is holding, while high-frequency discretionary spend (travel, online retail) is softening — which typically precedes a broader consumption deceleration by two to three quarters.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-06-05:growth: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline; the internals are less flattering. IIP capital goods at 12.5% is the one bright spot, but without capacity utilisation data to anchor it, the acceleration could reflect lumpy government orders rather than a private investment cycle turning. The GDP-GVA gap deserves scrutiny: when high-frequency proxies diverge this sharply from the headline, the deflator arithmetic usually explains it.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-06-05:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is benign on the surface, but the composition is doing the work — fuel at -3.78% is suppressing the headline while food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% and the FAO Food Price Index has risen to 128.5. regime_classification.2026-06-05:credit: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8% is a 300bps structural mismatch now in its third consecutive month of widening. The last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during an easing cycle was FY14; within 12 months, external shocks forced a policy reversal. Today's FX reserve buffer is larger, but the structural tension is identical.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-06-05:policy: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of roughly 200bps — restrictive by any measure, yet the RBI has not cut. The INR at the 91st percentile of 10-year weakness is the binding constraint the policy statement will not say aloud.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-06-05:capex: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% — up 820bps from the prior month — is the most eye-catching number in today's data, and the most suspect. Core sector growth fell to 2.3% from 4.7% in the same period; cement and steel, the physical embodiment of capex, are not corroborating the IIP signal. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable — a gap that, historically, tends to coincide with survey fatigue at the bottom of a utilisation cycle rather than the top. Vehicle sales are entirely consumption-led: PV +26.1%, 2W +28.7%, CV +28.9% — commercial vehicles are a capex proxy, but their surge reflects fleet replacement and logistics demand, not greenfield investment. Private capex acceleration requires utilisation above 75% as a precondition; without that data, the IIP print is a hypothesis, not a confirmation.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-06-05:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-06-05","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 cr is a record, but the composition matters more than the quantum: e-commerce GMV growth has decelerated to 12.4% from 25%, and airline passengers have fallen to a 10-year low of 35.08mn — two high-frequency demand proxies pointing to a mass-market consumption squeeze even as vehicle sales surge. PV sales at +26.1% and 2W at +28.7% are urban and semi-urban stories; the informal economy, which employs 85% of the workforce, is more visible in airline load factors and e-commerce basket sizes than in GST totals that capture formal-sector formalization. Retail mall vacancy at 5.7% — a decade low — confirms premium retail is tight, but office absorption at the 24th percentile and e-commerce deceleration suggest the mass consumer is pulling back precisely as the affluent consumer accelerates.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-06-06:growth: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% is the headline, but the quality check fails on two counts: core sector growth slumped to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% prior month — the weakest in 18 months — while PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, with manufacturing sub-index dropping to 53.9 from 56.9. regime_classification.2026-06-06:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% is benign, but the composition is doing work the headline conceals: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% — a 134bps move in one month — while core CPI holds flat at 3.4%. regime_classification.2026-06-06:credit: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% YoY against deposit growth of only 10.8% is a 300bps structural mismatch now in its third consecutive month of widening. regime_classification.2026-06-06:policy: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — the highest in the current cycle and well above the RBI's own neutral rate estimate of 1.0-1.5%. For housing, the average home loan rate has already fallen to 8.0% from 9.1%, and affordability index improved to 0.61 — but with INR at a 10-year weakness of 95.24, any rate cut that widens the India-US rate differential further risks accelerating FII outflows already running negative.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-06-06:capex: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY is a sharp jump from 4.3% — but core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal combined) is the more reliable proxy for actual investment activity, and it is at an 18-month low. regime_classification.2026-06-06:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-06-06","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2.01 lakh crore and PV sales up 26.1% YoY with two-wheeler sales up 28.68% look like a consumption boom — but the divergence with airline passengers at a 7th-percentile low and e-commerce GMV growth at a 24-month low of 12.4% is the tell. regime_classification.2026-06-27:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-06-27","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2,00,640 cr are a record, but the composition matters more than the total — April advance filings and ongoing formalisation of the informal economy can inflate the number without reflecting genuine demand acceleration. regime_classification.2026-06-28:growth: {"run_date":"2026-06-28","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"India's headline 7.8% GDP sits 90bps above the RBI's own 6.9% forecast — a gap that historically resolves downward, not up, once base effects wash out. regime_classification.2026-06-28:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-06-28","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% looks benign, but it moved 46bps higher from last month's 2.75% — and food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13%, a 134bps single-month acceleration. regime_classification.2026-06-28:credit: {"run_date":"2026-06-28","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — up from 82.5% last month and at the 81st percentile of its 10-year range — is the structural fault line beneath every bullish India narrative. regime_classification.2026-06-28:policy: {"run_date":"2026-06-28","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of roughly 200bps — tight enough to suppress private capex IRRs but not tight enough to arrest credit growth. signal_cards.2026-05-04:7: {"run_date":"2026-05-04","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Airline Pax Collapse Signals Demand Cliff","data_text":"Airline passenger traffic at 35.08 (7th percentile, extreme low, down from 38.59 last month) — a 9.1% MoM collapse. This is the sharpest drop in 18 months. Simultaneously, e-commerce GMV growth has decelerated to 12.4% from 25% YoY (15th percentile, weak). Yet GST collections remain strong at ₹200,640 cr YoY (71st percentile) and PV sales at 26.1% (56th percentile) are robust. The divergence is stark: discretionary travel (airlines) and online shopping (e-commerce) are crashing, but offline consumption (GST, vehicle sales) is holding. This suggests affluent consumers (who fly and shop online) are pulling back, while mass-market consumers (who buy vehicles and shop offline) are still spending. This is a classic late-cycle bifurcation — the wealthy are de-risking, the poor are still consuming.","implication":"This is a 6-month leading indicator of earnings downgrades for discretionary sectors (airlines, e-commerce, premium retail). Overweight mass-market plays: two-wheeler OEMs (Hero, Bajaj), commercial vehicles (Ashok Leyland, Tata Motors CV), and offline retail (Reliance Retail, Avenue Supermarts). Underweight premium discretionary: airline stocks (IndiGo, SpiceJet), e-commerce (Flipkart, Amazon India), and luxury goods. The airline collapse is NOT due to capacity constraints — it's demand destruction. If airline pax doesn't recover in April-May, expect a 15-20% earnings cut for aviation and hospitality sectors in FY27.","pct_10y":7,"pct_note":"Airline pax at 7th percentile is a 10-year low outside of COVID. The last time airline traffic fell this sharply (2020, 2022), it preceded a 3-6 month slowdown in discretionary spending and a 10-15% correction in consumption-linked stocks. This is a falsifiable signal: if airline pax rebounds to >38 by May 2026, the demand cliff thesis is wrong.","is_surprise":true}

SIP YoY growth decelerated to 15% from 17%, yet absolute inflows remain at ₹29,845 cr (85th percentile).","implication":"This is late-cycle behaviour — high-net-worth individuals are rotating into SIPs for tax efficiency and discipline, not new retail entering. Expect SIP account growth to remain muted (5-8% YoY) while AUM grows 18-22% YoY. Overweight large-cap, dividend-paying stocks where HNI SIP flows concentrate. Avoid micro-cap and small-cap funds — they'll see outflows as SIP money gravitates to index and large-cap schemes.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 82nd percentile has historically preceded 12-18 month market consolidations, not rallies. The last time SIP accounts grew this slowly (2nd percentile) was in 2022, before the 2023 correction.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-06-29:4: {"run_date":"2026-06-29","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"watch","title":"Oil Creep, But Demand Signals Weaken","data_text":"Brent crude at $73.43/bbl (55th percentile, neutral), up from $71.99 — a 2% move. WTI at $70.28 (58th percentile). Natural gas spiked to $3.305 (21st percentile, score: +58 — mild positive), up from $3.231. Copper at $6.194 (73rd percentile, score: +46), iron ore at $161.91 (63rd percentile). Yet airline passenger traffic collapsed to 35.08 (7th percentile, score: -86) and e-commerce GMV growth halved to 12.4% from 25% (15th percentile, score: -70).","implication":"Commodity prices are rising on supply tightness (OPEC+, geopolitical), not demand strength. India's import bill will rise 3-5% in Q4 FY26, pressuring CAD. Hedge crude exposure via long-dated puts; don't chase the rally. The collapse in airline traffic and e-commerce growth suggests demand destruction is real — oil will correct 8-12% within 8 weeks if demand data continues to soften. Avoid refiners; margins will compress.","pct_10y":55,"pct_note":"Brent at 55th percentile with collapsing demand signals (airline -86, e-com -70) is a classic divergence that preceded the 2020 and 2015 oil crashes. Supply-driven rallies without demand support typically reverse within 6-10 weeks.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-06-29:5: {"run_date":"2026-06-29","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"positive","title":"US Volatility Collapse Signals Risk-On","data_text":"US VIX at 18.41 (13th percentile, score: +74 — strong positive), down from 18.89. Nikkei collapsed to 6,936 (0th percentile, score: -100) — data error or BoJ intervention signal.","implication":"Global liquidity is abundant — VIX compression and S&P 500 at all-time highs signal carry trades are fully funded. The Nikkei collapse is likely a data error, but if real, it signals BoJ tightening — watch for JPY strength, which would unwind carry trades. Hedge via long JPY calls.","pct_10y":100,"pct_note":"S&P 500 and Euro Stoxx at 100th percentile with VIX at 13th percentile is a classic risk-on regime that has historically lasted 8-16 weeks before a 5-8% correction. This regime preceded the 2021 rally and the 2024 January-March surge.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-06-29:6: {"run_date":"2026-06-29","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR Weakness Masks RBI's Quiet Intervention","data_text":"INR/USD at 94.34 (87th percentile, score: -37 — mild negative), flat from prior. DXY at 101.388 (53rd percentile, neutral). FII equity net flows at -₹114 cr (49th percentile, neutral), a sharp reversal from +₹22,615 cr prior month. DII equity net at +₹128 cr (22nd percentile, score: -28). Gold in INR at ₹1,000/gram (0th percentile, score: -30), down from ₹1 — data anomaly, but signals INR strength vs commodity prices.","implication":"FII outflows of ₹114 cr suggest foreign investors are rotating out of India equities — likely profit-taking after the 77th percentile Nifty50 run. RBI is likely intervening to prevent INR from breaching 94.5, but this is a holding action. If FII outflows persist (>₹500 cr/month), INR will weaken to 95.5-96 within 6 weeks. Hedge INR exposure via 3-month forwards; don't fight RBI, but position for eventual 2-3% depreciation. Avoid INR-denominated bonds; yields don't compensate for FX risk.","pct_10y":87,"pct_note":"INR at 87th percentile weakness with FII outflows has historically preceded 4-6 week periods of 2-3% depreciation. The last time FII flows reversed this sharply was in March 2020 and September 2022, both followed by INR weakness.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-06-29:7: {"run_date":"2026-06-29","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Retail Malls Tightening While Offices Soften","data_text":"Retail mall vacancy at 5.7% (11th percentile, score: +78 — strong positive), up from 5.1% — a 60bps tightening in a market that's been oversupplied for 5 years. Simultaneously, office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft (24th percentile, score: -52 — mild negative) with vacancy at 13.9% (38th percentile, score: +24). This is a cross-domain divergence: retail is consolidating (fewer, larger malls), while office space is fragmenting (GCCs and co-working eating into traditional IT services real estate).","implication":"Retail real estate is entering a supply-constrained phase — expect 8-12% rental growth in Tier-1 malls over 18 months. Overweight listed retail REITs (Brookfield, Indiabulls) and anchor tenant stocks (Reliance, Titan). Office space is bifurcating: Grade-A (GCC-focused) will see 5-7% rental growth, but Grade-B/C (traditional IT services) will see 2-3% growth or compression. Avoid office REITs with >40% IT services exposure. The surprise: e-commerce GMV growth collapsed to 12.4% (15th percentile), yet retail mall vacancy is tightening — this means physical retail is cannibalizing e-commerce, not the reverse. This reversal of the 2015-2020 narrative is a 18-month structural shift.","pct_10y":11,"pct_note":"Retail mall vacancy at 11th percentile (tightening) while office at 24th percentile (loosening) is a 13-percentile divergence that has not occurred since 2015-16. This signals a structural reallocation of consumer spending from digital to physical, a reversal of the pandemic-era trend. Falsifiable: if e-commerce GMV growth rebounds to >20% within 2 quarters, this thesis breaks.","is_surprise":true}

executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty50 at 23,997 (+3.78%) and Sensex at 77,563 (+3.95%) are pricing the base case — not the bull case — which means reform acceleration or a credit resolution catalyst could drive a further 8–12% re-rating. Real estate presents a structural contradiction: home loan disbursements at ₹5,50,000 Cr are strong, avg rates fell 50 bps to 7.65%, yet RE sales declined 6.7% to 1,01,675 units against an affordability index deteriorating 32% to 0.61 — volume is being bought by rate cuts, not by income growth. Office REITs are the cleaner play: absorption at 83.3 (+13.7%) with vacancy compressing to 15.2% — Embassy REIT at ₹429.85 and Mindspace at ₹449.98 are at 75–79th percentiles, not stretched. Unsold residential inventory at 6,00,000 units caps developer pricing power → residential equity upside is limited without an affordability shock reversal.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: #1 — CD ratio at 83.04% with NBFC credit growing 15–17% on constrained deposits → funding cost spike → NBFC NIM compression → credit crunch cascade (bear scenario trigger, high probability, systemic impact). #2 — Affordability index at 0.61 (+32% deterioration) against 6,00,000 units unsold inventory; any rate reversal → mortgage stress → residential developer defaults (medium probability, concentrated impact). #3 — Copper at 95th percentile and iron ore at $161.91/t signal commodity re-inflation risk; Brent reverting toward $110 — CAD widens $25bn annually → INR pressure → RBI forced tightening → equity multiple compression. Watch the CD ratio print for April — at 83.04%, a further 50–100 bps widening triggers mandatory RBI macro-prudential intervention.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP growth with PMI composite at 56.5 is the headline; the subtext is a growth regime that is decelerating on every sub-indicator that matters — manufacturing PMI fell 300bps in a month to 53.9, core sector output dropped to 2.3% from 4.7%, and the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% sits 90bps below the statistical agency's print. GST at ₹2,00,640 crore — the first ₹2 lakh crore month — is real, but two-wheeler sales at 28.68% and CV sales at 28.89% YoY are doing more analytical work than the GST headline: rural and freight demand is genuinely recovering, not just being formalized. IIP capital goods at 12.5% would be unambiguously positive if core sector and cement dispatches corroborated it — they do not, which makes this a data point to verify, not celebrate. India's growth is real but narrowing: the formal economy is expanding, the informal economy is stabilizing, and the capex cycle is more promise than proof.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4%, a 370bps deceleration in a single quarter — is the most consequential global data point this week, yet S&P 500 at the 96th percentile and Nasdaq at an all-time high suggest equity markets have decided this is a soft patch, not a stall. US core PCE at 3.1%, ticking up from 3.0%, with Fed funds at 3.625% and the balance sheet expanding to $6,675bn, means the Fed is simultaneously easing liquidity while inflation re-accelerates — a combination that historically resolves with either a policy mistake or a growth shock. Brent at $98.06 and WTI at $99.67 — with iron ore surging 50% in a month to $161.91/tonne — suggest commodity markets are pricing a China demand revival that China's own GDP at 4.5% (down from 5%) does not yet justify. Global PMI composite at 51 — barely above contraction — with the US at 50.3 and Europe at sub-1% GDP growth means India's 7.8% is an outlier that will attract capital only if the INR holds.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

FII equity net flows swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month — from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — while DII support at +₹128 crore is at the 22nd percentile, meaning domestic institutions are not absorbing the FII exit with conviction. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, and SIP YoY growth has decelerated from 17% to 15% — the retail liquidity engine that cushioned every FII exit since 2022 is running at lower pressure. CD ratio at 83.04% — with credit at 13.8% and deposits at 10.8% — means the banking system's internal liquidity is being consumed faster than it is being replenished, forcing corporate bond issuance to ₹1,12,000 crore as a pressure valve. If RBI cuts 50bps as markets expect, the India-US rate differential compresses to 140bps at the precise moment FII outflows are accelerating — the INR at the 84th percentile has no margin for error.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 — 76th percentile — with Bank Nifty at the 90th percentile and India VIX rising to 20.4 from 19.7 creates a specific risk: banks are the most expensive segment of a market facing the most acute structural stress — CD ratio at 83%, NBFC deceleration, and potential macro-prudential tightening on unsecured lending. Real estate presents the sharpest internal contradiction in today's dataset: affordability at a decade-best 0.61, home loan rates at 8% (down from 9.1%), and ₹5,50,000 crore in disbursements — yet launches at an all-time low 0th percentile and sales declining to 1,01,675 units from 1,08,970, with unsold inventory at 6,00,000 units. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy compressing 330bps to 13.9% is the one clean positive — Bengaluru rents up 8.4% to ₹102/sqft — but Nifty IT flat YoY means the equity market is not pricing the physical demand that office REITs are experiencing. Embassy REIT at ₹437 and Mindspace at ₹450 — both near 79th percentile — are the better expression of the office thesis than IT equities.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent sustaining above $98 — every $10 adds ~$15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported food inflation feeds CPI with a two-quarter lag, directly threatening the rate-cut narrative and the INR stability that FII re-entry requires. Second, CD ratio breaching 84% — the level at which RBI has historically intervened with macro-prudential measures on risk weights — triggering NBFC funding costs to spike, credit growth to decelerate sharply, and consumption-linked equities to re-rate lower. Third, US core PCE at 3.1% and rising with GDP at 0.7% — stagflation is not the base case but it is now a 25% probability scenario — DXY strengthening from 98.9 would accelerate FII outflows from EM including India at the worst possible moment. Fourth, iron ore at $161.91 — a 50% monthly surge — if sustained, steel input costs rise, infrastructure project IRRs compress, and the capex cycle that IIP capital goods at 12.5% is supposed to signal gets deferred. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity data and weekly deposit growth figures — if the credit-deposit gap widens beyond 300bps for a third consecutive month, the bear scenario probability doubles.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP sits 90bps above the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% — a gap that demands a deflator audit, not a growth upgrade. PMI Composite at 56.5 is decelerating from 58.9, manufacturing sub-index has dropped 300bps to 53.9, and core sector at 2.3% — an 18-month low — is the ground-level rebuttal to the headline. GST at ₹2.006 lakh crore is a record, but e-commerce GMV growth halving to 12.4% and airline traffic falling 9% to a 7th-percentile low suggests the consumption engine is running on two cylinders, not four. Food inflation re-accelerating to 3.47% from 2.13% — with Brent at $96.44 threatening the fuel deflation buffer — means the benign CPI print of 3.21% has a shelf life measured in quarters, not years.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4% — is the sharpest single-quarter deceleration since Q1 2022, and US PMI Composite at 50.3 confirms this is not a data aberration. Fed funds at 3.625% with US core PCE at 3.1% (up from 3.0%) means the Fed's real rate is barely positive — insufficient to declare victory, insufficient to cut — a policy paralysis that keeps the dollar range-bound with DXY at 98.8. Iron ore's 50% surge to $161.91 on China stimulus expectations, alongside Hang Seng up 2.5% and China CPI recovering to 1.3% from 0.2%, suggests Beijing's reflation is gaining traction — which is simultaneously good for India's export demand and bad for India's commodity import bill. Global PMI at 51, down from 51.5, is stalling at the expansion threshold — not a recession signal, but a world that cannot absorb India's export ambitions at the pace the trade ministry projects.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its decade range — is the structural constraint that no rate cut can solve: deposit growth at 10.8% is simply not keeping pace with credit demand at 13.8%, and the gap is widening. FII equity net flows swung from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — a ₹22,729 crore reversal in a single period — with INR stability at 92.59 maintained only by visible RBI intervention, which depletes the reserve buffer that is India's CAD shock absorber. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore are declining from ₹31,002 crore, YoY growth decelerating to 15% from 17% — domestic equity liquidity is still positive but the marginal momentum is fading precisely when FII support has evaporated. Corporate bond issuance at the 7th percentile of its decade range signals that corporates are not accessing capital markets as a deposit-gap relief valve — they are rationing debt, which is a leading indicator of capex compression in 2-3 quarters.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 (76th percentile) and Bank Nifty at 54,822 (90th percentile) are pricing a benign outcome — but Bank Nifty's 90th-percentile valuation against an 81st-percentile CD ratio is a compression trade waiting to be triggered: NIM pressure from deposit rate competition → earnings downgrades → passive outflows from index funds that are overweight financials. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy collapsing to 13.9% from 17.2% and Bengaluru rents up 9% to ₹102/sqft is the most underappreciated positive in today's dataset — commercial real estate is tightening faster than equity markets have priced. Residential real estate shows a divergence: home loan rates falling to 8% have lifted the affordability index to 0.61, but sales units are down to 101,675 from 108,970 while unsold inventory has risen to 600,000 units — demand is rate-sensitive but supply is outrunning absorption. Gold in INR terms at ₹2,000/gram — an all-time high — is the market's verdict on INR trajectory and real return expectations: when gold is at the 84th percentile in USD and the 0th percentile (all-time high) in INR, the currency is doing the heavy lifting of the inflation story.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-10:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-10","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent sustaining above $95 — every $10 adds $15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported inflation feeds CPI with a 2-quarter lag, directly threatening the RBI's 4.6% inflation forecast and closing the rate-cut window entirely. Second, CD ratio breaching 84% — the level at which RBI macro-prudential action (risk weight hikes on unsecured and NBFC lending) becomes near-certain — triggering NBFC credit growth below 10% and a consumption credit squeeze that would expose the fragility beneath the GST record. Third, food inflation printing above 4% in May on the back of FAO Food Index at 128.5 and a below-normal pre-monsoon — a combination that forces RBI to hold rates through H1 FY27 even as growth decelerates toward the RBI's own 6.9% forecast. Fourth, FII outflow acceleration if US recession fears deepen — DXY at 98.8 is benign, but a US hard landing would reprice EM risk premia and test RBI's FX intervention capacity against a $96 oil import bill simultaneously. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity operations and the April CPI print — if food inflation exceeds 3.8%, the rate-cut consensus collapses and Bank Nifty's 90th-percentile valuation becomes the most exposed trade in the book.

"}

Second, food inflation persistence — CFPI at 3.47% (up from 2.13%) combined with FAO food index at 128.5 and rising closes the RBI's easing window faster than the rate market is pricing; a single monsoon disruption pushes CPI above 4.5% and the real rate argument for cuts evaporates. Third, US growth stall contagion — US GDP at 0.7% SAAR with PMI at 50.3 while equities are at the 96th-100th percentile is a compression trade waiting for a catalyst; an S&P correction of 10%+ would trigger FII equity outflows from India that the current ₹-114 crore daily run-rate does not yet reflect. executive_summary.2026-04-12:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-12","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP with a 56.5 PMI Composite reads as expansion until you pull the thread: core sector growth fell to 2.3% from 4.7%, PMI Manufacturing slid to 53.9 from 56.9, and the GDP-GVA statistical gap remains unresolved — the headline is a trapdoor. Food CPI's jump to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single month, against a flat core at 3.4%, means RBI's revised inflation forecast of 4.6% is already stale — the FAO food index at 128.5 has not yet fed through. India's dual-economy split is sharpening: vehicle sales across all three segments up 26-29% while airline traffic falls to a decade-low 7th percentile — the mass market is spending, the affluent consumer is not.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-27:2:
{"run_date":"2026-06-27","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP at 0.7% SAAR — down from 4.4% — while S&P 500 sits at an all-time high 7,354 and Euro Stoxx 50 at 6,221 (100th percentile) is the central contradiction in global macro: equity markets are pricing a recovery that output data is not delivering. US core PCE ticked to 3.1% from 3.0%, keeping the Fed pinned at 3.625% with the 10-year at 4.4% — a 79th-percentile yield that makes the carry trade expensive for EM allocators. China GDP at 4.5%, down from 5%, with CPI finally positive at 1.3%, suggests the deflation floor is in — but the global PMI Composite at 51.0 offers no acceleration thesis to justify current equity valuations.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-27:3:
{"run_date":"2026-06-27","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP inflows at ₹29,845 cr (85th percentile) are holding, but the account growth story has inverted: SIP accounts at 10.45 cr sit at the 2nd percentile of the decade, meaning AUM growth is being driven by ticket-size expansion among existing investors, not new retail participation. FII equity net flows at -₹114 cr are near-neutral, but the swing from last month's ₹22,615 cr inflow is a 100% reversal — the carry trade that funded that inflow is now priced at US 10Y 4.4% with no Fed cut in sight. DII absorption at ₹128 cr (22nd percentile) is too thin to absorb any FII selling of scale; the market's stability depends on SIP auto-debit continuity, not active allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-27:4:
{"run_date":"2026-06-27","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Bank Nifty at a 96th-percentile valuation with CD ratio at 83.04% and deposit growth trailing credit by 300bps is the equity market's most exposed position — NIM compression from deposit rate competition is the second-order effect the index is not pricing. Real estate shows a clean bifurcation: home loan rates down to 8% from 9.1% have lifted affordability (index 0.61), but RE sales fell to 1,01,675 units from 1,08,970 while unsold inventory rose to 6,00,000 units — the rate transmission is improving affordability without clearing supply. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft (24th percentile) with Bengaluru rents up 9.4% YoY and retail mall vacancy rising to 5.7% from 5.1% confirms the commercial real estate cycle is bifurcating by asset class, with Grade-A office holding while retail softens.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-27:5:
{"run_date":"2026-06-27","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Three risks, ranked by probability × impact: First, the CD ratio at 83.04% breaching 84% would trigger macro-prudential action — risk weights on unsecured lending or a deposit rate floor — with direct NIM and earnings consequences for the top-5 private banks. Second, food CPI at 3.47% accelerating through 4.5% by August would close the door on any FY27 rate cut and reprice the short end of the curve by 25-40bps, reversing the home loan affordability gain. Third, e-commerce GMV growth at 12.4% — already at the 15th percentile — combined with airline traffic at the 7th percentile suggests the affluent consumption engine is stalling; if PV sales follow with a 2-month lag (as they did in Q3 FY20), the GST revenue beat will not repeat. Watch Friday's RBI weekly data release on deposit mobilisation — if deposit growth does not close the gap toward 12%, the credit-deposit arithmetic forces a choice between slower lending or higher deposit rates, and neither is priced.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-28:1:

7.8% GDP against a core sector print of 2.3% — down from 4.7% — is a 550bps divergence between the statistical economy and the physical one. PMI Composite at 56.5 is decelerating across every sub-index: manufacturing fell to 53.9 from 56.9, services to 57.5 from 58.1 — the direction matters as much as the level. GST at ₹2.01 lakh crore is a record, but airline traffic at a 7th-percentile low and e-commerce GMV growth halved to 12.4% are volume proxies that the GST headline cannot override. India's formal economy is expanding; its volume economy is not — and that gap is where the next earnings revision cycle begins.

"}

Core PCE ticked up to 3.1% from 3.0% while US 10Y yields fell to 4.4% — the bond market is pricing disinflation that the Fed's preferred inflation gauge is actively contradicting. China GDP at 4.5% (down from 5.0%) and Hang Seng at a 43rd-percentile 22,672 confirm that the global growth engine is running below its advertised speed. Copper up 2.3% to $6.21/lb while Brent falls to $72.6 is the industrial metals market pricing a demand recovery that oil — the broadest demand proxy — is not corroborating.

Corporate bond issuance at ₹1,12,000 crore sits at the 7th percentile of its decade range — not a sign of strength but of banks crowding out the bond market as deposit shortfalls force balance-sheet management. India VIX at 13.05 — an 8th-percentile print — is pricing a calm that the liquidity data does not support.

Office absorption at 29.9 msf (up from 28.2) and vacancy falling to 13.9% from 17.2% is the one clean positive in the real estate complex — commercial is tightening while residential is softening. Retail mall vacancy at 5.7% — up from 5.1% — is a one-quarter leading indicator for consumer discretionary earnings, and it is moving in the wrong direction.

Food CPI at 3.47% — up 134bps in a single month — combined with a FAO food index at 128.5 (up from 125.3) means the base-effect tailwind on headline inflation expires in Q3 precisely when monsoon outcomes are still uncertain. US core PCE at 3.1% rising while the bond market prices Fed cuts creates a dislocation that resolves either in a sharp yield spike or a Fed communication reset — either outcome tightens the DXY and pressures INR from its current 94.34 level. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity data and the next fortnightly deposit growth print — if deposit growth does not show a 50bps sequential acceleration, the CD ratio story moves from structural concern to active policy trigger.

executive_summary.2026-06-29:1:
{"run_date":"2026-06-29","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP with a 56.5 PMI Composite is the headline; the fault lines are in the sub-indices. PMI Manufacturing fell to 53.9 from 56.9 — a 300bps drop in a single month — while core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7%, covering the eight infrastructure industries that proxy real physical output. IIP Capital Goods at 12.5% is the lone bright spot, but one month of strong machinery orders against a backdrop of decelerating cement, steel, and electricity output is a divergence, not a confirmation. India's FY estimate has been revised up to 7.6% from 7.1%, yet the RBI's own forecast sits at 6.9% — a 70bps gap between the government's optimism and the central bank's caution that the market has not fully priced.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-29:2:
{"run_date":"2026-06-29","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP at 0.7% SAAR — down from 4.4% — is the most important number in global macro right now, and it is being ignored by equity markets pricing S&P 500 at an all-time 7,354. US core PCE ticked up to 3.1% from 3.0%, narrowing the Fed's room to cut even as growth decelerates sharply; the bond market's 4.4% 10Y yield, falling 1bp, is not yet pricing the stagflation scenario that a 0.7% growth print with 3.1% PCE implies. China GDP at 4.5% (down from 5.0%) and Eurozone GDP at 0.9% (down from 1.2%) confirm the deceleration is synchronised, not idiosyncratic. The Nikkei print at 6,936 — flagged as a data anomaly — warrants monitoring; if it reflects BoJ intervention or a circuit-breaker event, the carry-trade unwind risk for INR and EM assets is non-trivial.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-29:3:

FII equity net flows swung to -₹114 crore from +₹22,615 crore the prior month — a reversal of nearly ₹23,000 crore that has not yet moved the Nifty, suggesting DII absorption is holding the index but at diminishing conviction: DII net at +₹128 crore is a 22nd-percentile print, well below the structural support levels of recent quarters. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore remain at the 85th percentile, but the account growth rate has decelerated to 15% YoY from 17%, and SIP accounts at 10.45 crore are at a 2nd-percentile growth rate — the retail base is not expanding, meaning the AUM growth is entirely ticket-size driven by existing, wealthier participants. Corporate bond issuance at ₹1,12,000 crore at the 7th percentile signals that the banking system's credit rationing is pushing corporates into the bond market at stress-level volumes — a precursor to spread widening if deposit mobilisation does not accelerate within two quarters.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-29:4:
{"run_date":"2026-06-29","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Bank Nifty at a 96th-percentile 58,177 while the CD ratio sits at 83.04% and NBFC credit growth decelerates to 13% from 17% is the classic divergence that precedes a risk-weight circular or a macro-prudential tightening — the regulator has the incentive and the data to act, and bank valuations have not priced that optionality. In real estate, the cross-signal is more nuanced: RE sales units fell to 1,01,675 from 1,08,970 even as home loan rates dropped to 8.0% from 9.1% — affordability improved but demand softened, which points to inventory overhang (unsold stock at 6,00,000 units) rather than rate sensitivity as the binding constraint. Retail mall vacancy tightened to 5.7% from 5.1% — an 11th-percentile reading signalling genuine scarcity in quality retail space — while office vacancy fell to 13.9% from 17.2%, driven by GCC expansion rather than traditional IT services recovery. The equity implication: real estate developers with retail exposure are better positioned than office-heavy portfolios where the vacancy improvement masks a structural tenant-mix shift.

"}
executive_summary.2026-06-29:5:
{"run_date":"2026-06-29","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, the CD ratio at 83.04% breaching 84% would trigger RBI macro-prudential action — risk weights on unsecured lending, NBFC funding restrictions — with a direct read-through to consumption credit and two-wheeler financing, the segment currently showing +28.7% sales growth. Second, food CPI at 3.47% accelerating further — FAO Food Index up to 128.5 from 125.3 — would close the door on any rate cut and force RBI to revise its 4.6% inflation forecast upward, repricing the short end of the curve by 25–50bps. Third, the FII reversal from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore in a single month, coinciding with INR at 94.34 and DXY at 101.4, leaves the rupee with limited buffer if US core PCE at 3.1% forces the Fed to hold longer than the market's current pricing implies. Watch this week: RBI's next liquidity management operation and any commentary on deposit mobilisation — that is the single data point that determines whether the credit-deposit stress resolves or cascades.

"}
